cross_system.title: Cross-system signal: credit_card flow softening ahead of stock
cross_system.body: Credit card spending flows are outpacing balance accumulation by 2-6x: transaction volumes grew 21-27% YoY in Apr 2026 while outstanding balances expanded just 3.8% in May 2026. Despite sustained payment growth, credit outstanding remains trapped in single digits for four consecutive periods, confirming the flow→stock lag effect where spending velocity leads balance build-up.

sibc.title: Co-lending & warehouse-financing infrastructure
sibc.body: Micro/small MSME lending accelerated 23.77pp in FY26, driving 30.09% YoY growth and capturing 23.12% of total MSME credit. Formalisation via UDYAM + GST makes underwriting scalable, but pure balance-sheet lending cannot match NBFC reach into informal segments. The 20.06pp size-spread compression signals market maturation—early infrastructure builders capture disproportionate origination flow.
sibc.implication: Build co-lending rails and warehouse-financing partnerships now while NBFC distribution remains fragmented and before competitors lock in the best origination channels.
chain: Registry + tax-data coverage (UDYAM, GST) makes small borrowers underwritable at scale for the first time; lenders with origination infrastructure convert registry access into credit at previously unachievable volumes.
Triggered by MSME formalisation via UDYAM registration + GST data — SIDBI MSME Pulse Special Edition Report, June 2025
Live signals confirming: sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration, sibc-msme-micro-small-share, sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy, sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread
sibc.driver: MSME formalisation via UDYAM registration + GST data

sibc.title: Gold loan market entry / deepening for banks
sibc.body: Gold loans surged 121.21% year-over-year with banks sustaining 8 consecutive periods of positive momentum, systematically taking market share from NBFCs as gold price appreciation expands secured lending capacity. Banks with existing branch networks hold decisive operational advantages in assay, custody, and cash management that NBFCs cannot replicate at scale. The growth trajectory remains structural—driven by collateral price inflation, not credit speculation—making this a durable revenue expansion opportunity.
sibc.implication: Deploy branch-based gold loan capabilities now while the structural tailwind persists and before NBFC incumbents can build competing operational infrastructure.
chain: Rising collateral value raises effective LTV headroom, enlarging the eligible borrower pool and the maximum loan per unit of collateral. Applies to any secured product against the same collateral, in any measure (stock or flow).
Triggered by Gold price surge (2023–2026) — RBI Press Releases – Sovereign Gold Bond Scheme (March 2023: prid=55327; Feb 202
Live signals confirming: sibc-pl-gold-pos-streak, sibc-pl-gold-yoy

sibc.title: MSME origination infrastructure investment window
sibc.body: Micro and small MSME credit accelerated 23.77pp in FY2026 to 30.09% YoY growth, now commanding 23.12% of total MSME portfolio share. The size-based growth spread compressed to 20.06pp as smaller borrowers outpaced medium enterprises, validating infrastructure-led origination models. Banks with UDYAM-GST underwriting rails are harvesting first-mover advantage in the formalised micro-borrower segment.
sibc.implication: Deploy MSME infrastructure investment within 24 months or accept structural underperformance as the formalisation wave matures and competitive positioning solidifies.
chain: Registry + tax-data coverage (UDYAM, GST) makes small borrowers underwritable at scale for the first time; lenders with origination infrastructure convert registry access into credit at previously unachievable volumes.
Triggered by MSME formalisation via UDYAM registration + GST data — SIDBI MSME Pulse Special Edition Report, June 2025
Live signals confirming: sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration, sibc-msme-micro-small-share, sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy, sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread
sibc.driver: MSME formalisation via UDYAM registration + GST data

sibc.title: PLI anchor-supplier chain finance
sibc.body: PLI capex drawdown is driving exceptional credit demand across anchor sectors—engineering credit surged 34.5% YoY while basic metals and chemicals sustained 17-20% growth through April 2026. These anchor companies are expanding supplier networks to meet PLI production targets, creating validated cash-flow relationships ripe for structured finance.
sibc.implication: Prioritize supply chain finance products targeting tier-1/tier-2 suppliers of PLI recipients over standalone MSME lending—the anchor backstop significantly de-risks the exposure while capturing the sectoral momentum.
chain: A capex incentive program (e.g. PLI) drives drawdowns first in upstream input sectors (metals, chemicals) and then downstream manufacturing (engineering) on a 2–3 year implementation lag — co-accelerating sectors that do not normally move together.
Triggered by PLI scheme capex drawdown phase (FY25–26) — PIB Press Note – PLI Scheme: Powering India's Industrial Renaissance, NoteId 155
Live signals confirming: sibc-basic-metal-sub-yoy-scan, sibc-chemicals-sub-yoy-scan, sibc-engineering-sub-yoy-scan

sibc.title: PSLC market depth from PSL reclassification surplus
sibc.body: PSL housing loans surged 39.94% YoY following the Oct 2024 ceiling revision, creating substantial reclassification surpluses at well-positioned banks. This one-time regulatory windfall generates sellable PSL Certificates for banks exceeding their revised targets. The influx of tradeable certificates deepens PSLC secondary market liquidity for the first time since the ceiling change.
sibc.implication: Buy PSLCs from surplus banks at favorable rates while supply peaks, or monetize your own reclassification gains before the market normalizes.
chain: Revising a PSL eligibility ceiling reclassifies existing sub-threshold stock into the priority bucket, producing reported growth that is not new lending and distorting the flow read.
Triggered by RBI PSL housing loan ceiling revision (Oct 2024) — RBI Master Direction – Priority Sector Lending (Targets and Classification), Ame
Live signals confirming: sibc-psl-housing-yoy
sibc.driver: RBI PSL housing loan ceiling revision (Oct 2024)

sibc.title: Renewable energy project finance — next capex wave
sibc.body: Infrastructure sub-sector scan shows traditional project finance declining (-26.03%, -44.06%, -11.91%) while renewable segments surge 74.02% year-over-year. The infrastructure supercycle's completion creates a $2T refinancing opportunity as project finance teams pivot from roads and bridges to solar farms and green hydrogen. Mixed signals across infrastructure (21.47% active, 1.41% strengthening, 0.45% stable) confirm the sector rotation underway.
sibc.implication: Redeploy existing project finance capabilities to renewable energy — same risk frameworks, higher regulatory tailwinds, deeper government pipeline visibility.
chain: As a project cohort moves from construction (drawdown) to operations (repayment), net credit balance declines unless new origination offsets it — suppressing growth even while existing books remain large.
Triggered by Infrastructure supercycle completing (FY22–24) — RBI SIBC merged series (Jan 2024 – Apr 2026) — Infrastructure sub-sector YoY
Live signals confirming: sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan
sibc.via: Project lifecycle phase → new origination vs repayment

sibc.title: Services sector credit entry / scale-up
sibc.body: Services credit surged 18.58% YoY with 6.99pp FY acceleration, now commanding 28.21% of total credit as NBFC intermediation rebounds (16.68% sub-sector growth, with specialized segments hitting 57.1%). The sector's outperformance across trade, CRE, and professional services creates a bifurcated credit market where services-exposed lenders capture disproportionate growth.
sibc.implication: Build direct services origination capabilities or establish NBFC partnerships to access the intermediated flow before competitors lock up the channel.
chain: Changes to bank-to-NBFC exposure risk weights expand or suppress the wholesale funding channel; recovery amplifies all NBFC-intermediated lending, which is reported under services regardless of end-use sector.
Triggered by NBFC credit channel recovery post risk-weight cycle — RBI Circular DOR.STR.REC.57/21.06.001/2023-24 (November 16, 2023)

Live signals confirming: sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan, sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count, sibc-services-fy-acceleration, sibc-services-share, sibc-services-share-scan, sibc-services-yoy, sibc-services-yoy-scan
sibc.driver: NBFC credit channel recovery post risk-weight cycle

sibc.title: EV-specific vehicle credit products
sibc.body: RETIRED at first FOUNDATION (2026-06-10). No external source verified for EV share of vehicle loans or EV-specific credit risk differentiation. No L1 signal separating EV from ICE vehicle loans. Returns only when EV share of vehicle loans is measurable in L1 (e.g., VAHAN registration data ingestion).
chain: Live signals confirming: sibc-pl-vehicle-share, sibc-pl-vehicle-yoy

sibc.title: Infrastructure repayment wave with insufficient new origination pipeline
sibc.body: Banks over-indexed to traditional infrastructure credit face a structural squeeze: the FY22-24 project book moves to repayment (compressing balances) while the next-cycle pipeline (renewables, data centres) is slower to ramp. Banks without next-cycle project finance capability face credit portfolio contraction.
sibc.implication: Lenders aligned to Project lifecycle phase → new origination vs repayment capture this first.
chain: As a project cohort moves from construction (drawdown) to operations (repayment), net credit balance declines unless new origination offsets it — suppressing growth even while existing books remain large.
Triggered by Infrastructure supercycle completing (FY22–24) — RBI SIBC merged series (Jan 2024 – Apr 2026) — Infrastructure sub-sector YoY
Live signals confirming: sibc-infra-sub-share-scan, sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan
sibc.via: Project lifecycle phase → new origination vs repayment

sibc.title: CIC reporting compliance gaps creating off-balance-sheet credit risk
sibc.body: RBI Digital Lending Guidelines (2022/2025) require BNPL and digital credit to report to Credit Information Companies. However, compliance may be incomplete among newer digital lenders operating under first-loss guarantee structures or co-lending arrangements where the regulated entity's on-book exposure is partially shielded. If reporting gaps exist, the true unsecured credit stress is understated in bank balance sheets and in SIBC data.
sibc.implication: Lenders aligned to Regulatory capital weight → unsecured origination economics capture this first.
chain: Higher risk weights raise the regulatory capital cost per unit of exposure, compressing origination economics for unsecured retail products until reversed.
Triggered by RBI risk-weight hike on unsecured credit (Nov 2023) — RBI Circular RBI/2023-24/89, 'Risk Weights for Consumer Credit and Credit Card R
Live signals confirming: sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak, sibc-pl-cc-share, sibc-pl-cc-yoy, sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak, sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share, sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy
sibc.driver: RBI risk-weight hike on unsecured credit (Nov 2023)
sibc.via: Regulatory capital weight → unsecured origination economics

atm_pos.title: Credit card penetration deepening — PSB share gap is the opening
atm_pos.body: Credit cards outstanding hit 119.4M in April 2026, growing 8.15% YoY, but PSBs capture only 23.8% share versus their dominant debit card position. Private banks command 71% of the credit card market, creating a 40+ percentage point share gap that PSBs can exploit through targeted debit-to-credit upgrades within their existing customer base. The acquisition economics favor upgrading known customers over cold prospecting in a market adding ~9M new cards annually.
atm_pos.implication: PSBs should prioritize credit card conversion campaigns targeting their debit card holders, leveraging existing relationship data and transaction history to drive higher-margin product adoption.
chain: Live signals confirming: cc-bank-scan, cc-category-share-scan, cc-outstanding-abs, cc-outstanding-yoy, cc-private-share, cc-psb-share

atm_pos.title: Rural cash distribution via Micro ATM / Business Correspondent networks
atm_pos.body: The micro ATM network declined 7.17% YoY to 1.37 million units, signaling sector consolidation as weaker operators exit rural cash distribution. Despite contraction, the 1.37M installed base represents persistent cash demand in markets where full ATM deployment remains uneconomical.
atm_pos.implication: Acquire micro ATM capacity from exiting players to consolidate rural distribution at discounted asset values.
chain: Live signals confirming: micro-atm-yoy, micro-atms-abs

atm_pos.title: Embedded transit and tap-to-pay payment products
atm_pos.body: The 'other' credit card category exploded 493% by value and 300% by volume YoY, signaling the emergence of embedded payment infrastructure beyond traditional merchant acceptance. This structural shift — whether transit NFC or RuPay-credit-on-UPI — is creating entirely new card-spend surfaces at platform scale. The growth rates indicate infrastructure rollout, not pilot testing.
atm_pos.implication: Identify and integrate with the winning embedded payment platform now, before the land-grab window closes and interchange economics get competed away.
chain: A new embedded payment use case (transit NFC tap-to-pay or RuPay-credit-on-UPI) creates a structurally growing card-spend channel in the unclassified 'other' category. Driver composition is unresolved (hypothesis).
Triggered by Embedded card payment infrastructure rollout (transit NFC or RuPay-credit-on-UPI)
Live signals confirming: cc-other-val-yoy, cc-other-vol-yoy
atm_pos.driver: Embedded card payment infrastructure rollout (transit NFC or RuPay-credit-on-UPI)
atm_pos.via: Embedded payment rails → new card-spend surface

atm_pos.title: Credit card market concentrated in top-5 private banks
atm_pos.body: The top-5 private banks control approximately 71% of credit card outstanding. The largest single issuer holds ~20% of the market. This concentration creates systemic fragility — as demonstrated when a single payment bank's exit caused a market-wide debit card step-down. Similar disruption from a major credit card issuer would have proportionally larger effects.

atm_pos.title: Debit card base structural decline reducing cash access for underbanked
atm_pos.body: The debit card base is contracting through payment bank exits and lifecycle purges. This reduces the potential transaction base for populations that depend on debit cards for cash access and digital payments — particularly lower-income and rural segments without credit cards or reliable UPI-linked accounts.
atm_pos.implication: Lenders aligned to Enforcement-driven institutional exit → card stock capture this first.
chain: Regulatory wind-down of a major issuer forces cancellation of its entire card portfolio, producing a single-period step-down in the stock concentrated in that issuer's lender category.
Triggered by RBI enforcement action against Paytm Payments Bank (Jan–Apr 2026) — RBI Press Release 2024-2025/1589 — Paytm Payments Bank Limited: directions under
Live signals confirming: dc-bank-scan, dc-category-share-scan, dc-outstanding-abs, dc-outstanding-yoy, dc-private-share, dc-psb-share
atm_pos.driver: RBI enforcement action against Paytm Payments Bank (Jan–Apr 2026)

atm_pos.title: Payment acceptance bifurcation: POS contracting while UPI grows
atm_pos.body: POS terminal network contraction while UPI QR expands creates an acceptance gap: merchants who drop POS terminals cannot accept international cards, EMI transactions, premium credit card features, or POS-authenticated payments. Customers without UPI-linked accounts face reduced options at merchants who have switched fully to UPI.
atm_pos.implication: Lenders aligned to Zero MDR → acceptance-infrastructure mix capture this first.
chain: Zero MDR on UPI makes QR acceptance structurally cheaper than card POS (no hardware, no per-txn fee), driving QR deployment while suppressing POS-terminal investment. Polarity is per-target: + for QR, − for POS.
Triggered by Zero MDR policy on UPI transactions (NPCI/GoI) — Union Budget 2022-23 — MDR waiver provisions for UPI and RuPay; NPCI product ove
Live signals confirming: pos-bank-scan, pos-category-share-scan, pos-private-share, pos-psb-share, pos-terminals-abs, pos-terminals-pos-streak, pos-terminals-yoy, upi-qr-per-pos, upi-qr-yoy
atm_pos.driver: Zero MDR policy on UPI transactions (NPCI/GoI)
